reasoning:
As of November 9, 2025, the Federal Reserve has enacted exactly two 25 basis point rate cuts in 2025 (on September 17 and October 29), bringing the target range from 4.25%-4.50% down to 3.75%-4.00% (sources: CNBC, AA). The Federal Open Market Committee has only one meeting remaining in the year, scheduled for December 10, 2025.

For this market to resolve to "Yes," there must be EXACTLY four 25 basis point cuts by the end of the year. Since there have only been two cuts so far, the Fed would have to implement an additional 50 basis point reduction (which counts as two 25 bps cuts according to the resolution criteria) at the December meeting. 

According to CME's FedWatch tool as of early November 2025, the market prices a ~64-68% probability of a 25 basis point cut in December and ~32-36% odds of no cut. Following the October 29 meeting, Fed Chair Powell noted that even a single 25 basis point cut in December "is not a foregone conclusion." With strong economic resistance to further aggressive easing, a 50 basis point "jumbo" cut in December would require an unforeseen, severe economic deceleration within a span of ~4 weeks. Such an event has a very low base rate. Therefore, the probability of reaching exactly 4 cuts total in 2025 (i.e., a 50 bps cut in Dec) is essentially negligible, bottoming out at the lower bound of 0.02.
